skill.name: expo-build-deploy
skill.description: Guides Expo React Native iOS build and release workflows with EAS Build, TestFlight, App Store submission, credentials, updates, and troubleshooting steps.
skill.summary: Guide Expo iOS apps from EAS setup through TestFlight and App Store release.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, environment, and heuristic issues, but the reviewed file is documentation rather than executable code. The critical obfuscation and weak-crypto findings are false positives; the real risk is that the guidance can lead an assistant to run account-changing EAS commands and handle API keys, so publication should include warnings.

medium_findings.title: Account-Changing EAS Commands in Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents EAS build, credential, submit, update, and secret commands. These are legitimate for an Expo deployment skill, but if an assistant runs them without explicit user confirmation they can alter credentials, upload builds, submit apps, publish updates, or list secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands are explicit EAS CLI operations with deployment, credential, update, and secret-management effects. The file is documentation, so the risk depends on whether an assistant executes the commands without user approval.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Key Handling Could Expose Client Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill shows creating an API_KEY secret and then placing process.env.API_KEY into Expo app config extra values. No real credential is present, but the pattern can expose sensitive API keys to client-side application code or shell history if copied directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential references are visible in the documented workflow and include an API key placeholder. This is not credential theft, but the configuration pattern is a real security footgun for sensitive server-side keys.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to example API endpoints and an App Store Connect URL used for instructions. No evidence found of data exfiltration, hidden network calls, or malicious domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are shown inside configuration examples or explanatory prose. They are not executable code and do not send local data anywhere.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Signals Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak-cryptography and system-reconnaissance patterns, but the reviewed lines are Expo release documentation, permission strings, checklist text, or placeholder build IDs. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, host probing, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal release-management prose at the cited locations. There is no code path that performs weak cryptography or local system reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Deployment CLI Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands that can build, submit, update, inspect, or cancel Expo iOS releases. These commands should be treated as user-approved operational actions, not automatic background steps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present and have real release workflow effects. Their presence is expected for the skill purpose, but they require execution safeguards.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Secret Management Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes EAS credential and secret commands. These commands can expose, modify, or create sensitive deployment material when run in a real project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly reference EAS credentials and secrets. The skill does not hide or exfiltrate them, but the operations are sensitive by nature.

content.user_title: Deploy Expo iOS Apps with EAS
content.value_statement: iOS release setup can be slow and error-prone. This skill guides EAS builds, TestFlight submission, credentials, OTA updates, and release troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Configure EAS build profiles for development, preview, and production iOS builds.
Explain app.json iOS settings, bundle identifiers, permissions, and build numbers.
Guide EAS credential setup, automatic signing, manual credentials, and local builds.
Show TestFlight and App Store submission workflows using EAS Submit.
Outline OTA update channels, update publishing, and runtime update checks.
Troubleshoot signing errors, compliance warnings, processing delays, and update channel issues.
limitations: Focuses on iOS workflows and does not cover full Android release setup.
Requires valid Expo, Apple Developer, and App Store Connect access.
Examples use placeholders that must be adapted before use.
Does not audit application code, privacy policy content, or backend key security.

use_cases.title: Prepare a First iOS Release
use_cases.description: Set up EAS profiles, app identifiers, versioning, and production build settings for a new Expo app.

use_cases.title: Ship an Internal TestFlight Build
use_cases.description: Build a release candidate, submit it to App Store Connect, and prepare tester access.

use_cases.title: Resolve Release Pipeline Failures
use_cases.description: Diagnose signing, compliance, build processing, local build, and OTA update problems.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up iOS Build Profiles
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Expo project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure EAS Build for an Expo iOS app. Review my current app settings and explain the required development, preview, and production profiles.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare TestFlight Submission
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through building and submitting my Expo iOS app to TestFlight. Ask before any command that changes my Expo or Apple account.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Signing or Credential Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: My EAS iOS build is failing with a signing or credential error. Help me inspect the likely cause and propose the least risky fix.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Production Release Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced App Store and OTA release process
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production release workflow for my Expo iOS app, including build numbers, submit profiles, OTA channels, secrets, and rollback considerations.

output_examples.input: I need to submit my first Expo iOS build to TestFlight.
output: Checklist for Expo account login, EAS configuration, bundle identifier, Apple team details, and build profile readiness.
Recommended build and submit sequence with reminders to confirm account, app ID, and target environment before execution.
Follow-up checks for App Store Connect processing, internal tester access, and unique build numbers.

output_examples.input: My production EAS build has a signing error.
output: Diagnosis path for certificate, provisioning profile, bundle identifier, and Apple team mismatch issues.
Credential reset options with warnings about account impact and suggested confirmation before changes.
Verification steps before starting another production build.

output_examples.input: I want to publish an OTA update safely.
output: Assessment of whether the change is JavaScript or asset-only and eligible for OTA release.
Branch and channel checks to avoid sending preview changes to production users.
Post-release validation steps for update availability and cold-start behavior.

best_practices: Confirm the Expo account, Apple team, bundle identifier, and release profile before running build or submit commands.
Keep server-only secrets out of client app config and use EAS secrets only for appropriate build-time values.
Increment build numbers for every App Store upload and test OTA channel routing before production release.
anti_patterns: Running build, submit, credential, or update commands without confirming the target account and environment.
Copying privileged API keys into Expo extra values that client application code can read.
Reusing production credentials or update channels for preview builds without a clear release policy.

faq.question: Does this skill run EAS commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. Users should approve account-changing commands before execution.

faq.question: Can it help with Android builds?
faq.answer: Only indirectly. The documented workflow focuses on iOS, TestFlight, App Store Connect, and iOS signing.

faq.question: Does it manage Apple certificates for me?
faq.answer: It explains EAS automatic and manual credential workflows, but valid Apple Developer access is still required.

faq.question: Can I use it for production App Store submissions?
faq.answer: Yes, it covers production builds and EAS Submit. Confirm app identifiers, credentials, and metadata before submitting.

faq.question: How should API keys be handled?
faq.answer: Use EAS secrets carefully, and never expose server-only keys through client-readable app configuration.

faq.question: What problems can it troubleshoot?
faq.answer: It covers signing failures, missing compliance warnings, stuck TestFlight processing, local builds, and OTA update issues.
